data.source: Online behavioral experiments with human participants (Amazon Mechanical Turk) and computational model simulations (ALCOVE-RL).
data.wave_or_subset: Experiment 1 (bee category-learning task); Experiment 2 (job application task); multiple simulation conditions (contingent/full information, random-info, constructivist coding; individuation, occlusion, noisy).
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 101 participants. Experiment 2: 400 participants recruited, 40 excluded (360 analyzed). Simulations: 1,000 runs per condition in one set; 20 learning blocks plus test for intervention simulations.
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant decisions and strategy scores across trials/blocks; simulated agent runs.
data.notes: Feedback conditions varied: contingent-information (feedback only for approached/accepted items) vs full-information (feedback on all items). Interventions in Experiment 2 included individuation, occlusion, and added noise. Simulation parameters included specificity constant c=6, temperature τ=15, and learning rates of 0.1 for output weights and attention.

method.description: The study combined computational simulations and human experiments to investigate attentional learning traps in category learning and decision-making tasks. Simulations used a reinforcement-learning adaptation of the ALCOVE model (ALCOVE-RL) to evaluate how selective attention under different feedback regimes leads to persistent mislearning. Human experiments compared contingent-information versus full-information feedback and tested interventions (individuation, occlusion, noise) on participants’ ability to learn a two-dimensional decision rule.
steps: Specify a four-feature category-learning environment requiring a two-dimensional rule to maximize rewards.

Simulate agents with the ALCOVE-RL model under multiple feedback conditions (contingent, full-information, random-info) and with/without selective attention; include constructivist coding manipulation.
Run additional simulations introducing individuation, occlusion, and noisy outcomes; train over learning blocks and evaluate in a test block without learning updates.
Experiment 1: Recruit 101 MTurk participants; administer a bee classification task with learning (64 trials) and surprise test phases (32 trials) under contingent vs full-information feedback.
Measure approach rates, adherence to 2D rule (2D score) and 1D trap (1D score), and inferred environment statistics; analyze differences using Bayesian parameter estimation.

Experiment 2: Recruit 400 MTurk participants (exclude 40); administer a job application task with five conditions (contingent, full-information, individuation, occlusion, noisy) over 128 learning trials and a 32-trial test phase.
Evaluate acceptance rates, adoption of correct 2D strategies, prevalence of 1D traps, and perceived base rates; analyze with Bayesian multilevel modeling and specified priors.
method.models: ALCOVE-RL (selective attention reinforcement learning model); Bayesian parameter estimation and Bayesian multilevel models. For continuous outcomes: normal likelihood with normal prior on the mean and truncated normal prior on SD. For binary outcomes: beta distribution with reparameterized mean and precision priors.
method.outcome_variable: Strategy adherence scores (2D score for correct rule; 1D score for trap), approach/acceptance rates, estimated proportion of bad/unsuitable items, proportion identifying correct feature combination.
method.independent_variables: Feedback condition (contingent-information vs full-information); information sampling (random-info); attention mechanism (attentional vs no-attention in simulations); constructivist coding; interventions (individuation, occlusion, noisy outcomes); learning block/trial.
method.control_variables: Learning block/trial; task stimuli features; in simulations, parameter settings (specificity, temperature, learning rates).

results.summary: Simulations showed that selective attention under choice-contingent feedback frequently led to learning traps, whereas full information or removing selective attention reduced traps and improved rule learning. In human experiments, full-information participants more often adopted the correct two-dimensional strategy and showed fewer learning-trap behaviors than contingent-information participants. Constructivist coding amplified traps in simulations. Interventions (individuation, occlusion, noise) improved performance in simulations over the long run but did not meaningfully reduce traps in Experiment 2 and often hindered learning. Participants under contingent information overestimated the prevalence of bad/unsuitable options and were less likely to identify the correct feature combination.
